notes: Petitioners and clients in Dante's office on the bank. The Emperor's visitors usually want something put in writing — a ruling, a witness, a stamped paper. They are not patient; they have business elsewhere. The brass clock from '62 ticks under all of them. The dish of hard candies has been on the desk since 1971; nobody's ever finished it.

visitors.name: The Petitioner
visitors.lore_text: Came in at opening time. Folder under their arm — a brown manila with a number in Dante's father's hand in the corner, '67 vintage. Has a specific question, the kind of question that gets answered in eight minutes if the office is in its right mind. They've been three to a smaller office downriver and they have not been heard. They want to be heard once, properly.

steps.greet: They nod from the anteroom chair. The folder is on their lap. They have been holding it on their lap for thirty-eight minutes; you can see the dent in the cardboard from their thumb.
steps.listen: They state the matter. It is short. It is clear. They speak the way a person speaks when they have rehearsed the sentence in a parking lot, twice, in their car.
steps.deliver: You bring the relevant statute book from the law shelf — volume 7 of the river-code, the one with the broken spine. The page they need has been earmarked since '94 by somebody who is no longer in this profession.
steps.sit_with: You write the ruling on the cream stock. You stamp it. The stamp leaves a perfect circle, the way it has for forty years. They thank you and stand up and they have the look of a person who is going to drive home the long way.

visitors.name: The Old Friend
visitors.lore_text: Someone Dante has known since they were both seven, in the same one-room school on the bank, with the same one teacher who is in the ground now. Comes to the office because the office is where Dante is. Has not actually needed a ruling in eleven years; needs a friend with a stamp, which is a different thing. Brings a story Dante has not heard in two decades and brings it without flourishes.

steps.greet: They sit in the left client chair before you offer it. The right chair has the better cushion. They know this and they will not take the better chair.
steps.listen: They catch you up on a person from forty years ago — the kid who broke his arm falling out of a tree behind the schoolhouse in '76 and never came back to school. They know what he does now. They tell you he is fine.
steps.deliver: You bring out the brandy. The bottle has been in the bottom-right drawer since Dante's father; it is the bottle the office runs on. You pour them half a glass. They take it in two sips and they put the glass down.
steps.sit_with: You sit with them at the client chairs. They have not been to your office in ten years. They will not stay long today. The brass clock ticks. It will be the only sound in the office for about forty seconds and the forty seconds will not be awkward.

visitors.name: The Grieving Widow
visitors.lore_text: Three days after the funeral. Here to get the estate filed. The folder she brings is in her husband's hand and the hand is the hand of a man who knew he was sick and wrote everything down. She is patient with the office; less patient with herself. She has cried twice today already, both times in her car, both times briefly.

steps.greet: She has the folder. The folder has dividers in his hand: HOUSE, CAR, ACCOUNTS, THE THING ABOUT THE BOAT. The last divider is the one she has not opened yet.
steps.listen: She tells you what he meant by each page. Some of the pages have annotations in his hand and some of the annotations are jokes. She reads the jokes without laughing and you do not laugh and the not-laughing is mutual respect.
steps.deliver: You bring out the estate-filing form. The form is the one the river-code requires; you have signed three hundred of them. You fill in the parts she can't fill in. You put a hard candy from the 1971 dish next to her elbow. She does not take it but she sees it.
steps.sit_with: You stamp the final page. She does not stand up immediately. You sit with her until she does. The brass clock has ticked through nine minutes since she walked in. She thanks you. She forgets to take her copy of the form. You walk it out to her car.

visitors.name: The Appellant
visitors.lore_text: Lost a case six months ago in another office, two towns downriver. They came here because Dante's office takes appeals on Tuesdays and today is a Tuesday and they checked the door three times to be sure. They have spent six months being angry; they are no longer angry. They are determined, which is the cleaner energy.

steps.greet: They will not sit. You let them stand. Some people argue better standing. Dante's father taught him to let them.
steps.listen: They present the case. The case is good. The original ruling was bad. You can hear from the second sentence that they have made this case to themselves in the mirror seven hundred times and that the case has gotten tighter each time.
steps.deliver: You pull the precedent off the shelf — volume 8 of the river-code, the misfiled folder from 1971 your father slid into the estate section by accident. The case they need is on page 134. It has been on page 134 the whole time. You hand them the page.
steps.sit_with: You write the appellate finding on the heavier stock the office reserves for these. You stamp it with the bronze date-wheel stamp. The stamp comes down hard. The appellant cries briefly, the kind of crying that comes out of the chest only. They thank you. They leave through the front door because they will not enter or exit through the alley.

visitors.name: The Clerk
visitors.lore_text: Dante's clerk. She has been here for nineteen years. She came through the alley door her first day in '95 because she didn't know where the front door was and she has come through the alley door ever since because by year three it was a habit and by year ten it was a position. Her throat-clearing is the office's metronome. She knows where every paper that has ever been in this office is.

steps.greet: She is at the cabinet before she says good morning. The cabinet drawer C-K is open. She is putting yesterday's CLERMONT v. ALCALA where it goes.
steps.listen: She tells you what's filed and what isn't. She tells you the appellant who hasn't arrived yet is going to need volume 8, and she says it with the certainty of a person who looked at the docket at 6:15 AM with coffee in their other hand.
steps.deliver: You bring her coffee. Black, two spoons of sugar she'd never admit to. The mug has DANTE LAW · ESTATE PLANNING · 555-0179 printed on it, from a print run in '88 that you have never run out of.
steps.sit_with: You sit at the desk while she files. The office is the office it should be. She clears her throat at the third shelf, where the river-code volumes 9 and 10 are missing. You both look at the gap. Neither of you mentions it. You haven't mentioned it in nine years.

visitors.name: Nicola (visiting)
visitors.lore_text: Comes over from the riverboat — three doors down. She does this when the boat has settled enough that she trusts it to settle without her for forty minutes. She brings a small jar from the pantry, usually. Today it is a honey she made from the upper-deck thyme she let go to flower in late August.

steps.greet: She knocks once and lets herself in. She has been letting herself in this way since '03.
steps.listen: She tells you about the rosebush. The rosebush has put out a bud overnight that it should not have, on the kind of cane that doesn't bud in February. She is not asking you to do anything about it; she is reporting it.
steps.deliver: You bring her one of the office's hard candies, from the dish on the desk that has been there since '71. She takes a butterscotch. She always takes a butterscotch. You have suspected for years that the candies are restocking themselves.
steps.sit_with: You sit at the desk with her at the client chair. The clock ticks. The radiator clicks. Through the window you can see the boat's mast tip. The boat is fine; the boat will be fine for forty minutes more than she gave it.

visitors.name: Dante (off-duty)
visitors.lore_text: When the player is not Dante, he comes in off-duty for a moment — through the alley door, the way the clerk comes in, because there is a kind of arrival that the front door is not for. When the player IS Dante, this entry swaps out for whoever is at the desk that night.

steps.greet: He hangs his jacket on the same hook he always uses. The hook has his shape worn into the wood where his shoulder lands.
steps.listen: He talks about a case from last week that doesn't have a folder yet. The case is in his head the way a case is in his head while it is still a case and not yet a record. He talks himself through it.
steps.deliver: You hand him a pen — the Bic the clerk reorders in twelve-packs. He thanks you. He has been thanking the clerk for pens for nineteen years and he means it every time.
steps.sit_with: You sit at the client chair while he sits at the desk. He stamps something. The office is the office. The brass clock ticks. The hard candy dish hums in its small ceramic way.
